intro: The clearest split between WebAIM and Assistiv Labs is cost: WebAIM is free to use, while Assistiv Labs sells a subscription with a free tier (entry price not public). WebAIM is nonprofit web accessibility training, free resources, and the WAVE evaluation tool. Assistiv Labs is remote assistive-technology testing platform (screen readers, voice control, switches). Pick WebAIM when the job is any team that needs reliable a11y reference material and the free wave checker and you accept low lock-in; pick Assistiv Labs when it is distributed qa and a11y engineers testing with screen readers remotely and you accept medium lock-in. The data we have shows WebAIM at fully public pricing and documentation with solid developer experience, and Assistiv Labs at some public pricing / roadmap detail with average developer experience. The honest trade-off: neither is universal — WebAIM is a poor fit for enterprises wanting a single vendor-managed monitoring platform, and Assistiv Labs is a poor fit for teams wanting automated wcag scans or monitoring dashboards. Match the pricing model and lock-in level to how your team actually works, not the.
quick_take: WebAIM is for nonprofit web accessibility training; Assistiv Labs is for remote assistive-technology testing platform (screen readers; decide based on budget vs. support.
verdict_a: Choose WebAIM if cost is the binding constraint and the work maps to nonprofit web accessibility training, free resources, and the WAVE evaluation tool.
verdict_b: Choose Assistiv Labs if some ecosystem coupling is acceptable and the work lines up with remote assistive-technology testing platform (screen readers, voice control, switches).

faq.q: Is WebAIM cheaper than Assistiv Labs?
faq.a: Neither WebAIM nor Assistiv Labs publishes entry pricing that lets us compare directly — WebAIM uses a free model and Assistiv Labs uses a subscription model. Get quotes from both before assuming one is cheaper.

faq.q: Can I migrate from WebAIM to Assistiv Labs?
faq.a: Migration is possible in either direction. WebAIM carries low lock-in and Assistiv Labs carries medium lock-in, so plan for meaningful but not blocking rework. Run both in parallel before you fully cut over.

WebAIM scores higher on developer experience in our data (4/5 vs 3/5). That reflects public feedback on docs, onboarding, and day-to-day ergonomics. Still, run a spike with your own code — DX ratings don't capture every edge.

faq.q: Is Assistiv Labs a good alternative to WebAIM?
faq.a: Yes — Assistiv Labs is a reasonable alternative to WebAIM for any team that needs reliable a11y reference material and the free wave checker.. The practical differences are free-vs-subscription billing and low-vs-medium lock-in. If those fit your constraints better, treat Assistiv Labs as a credible swap.
